Hmm, dynamic programming seems like a good approach here. Because for each die, the number of ways depends on the previous dice. Let's think of a DP table.

Let me define dp[i][t] as the number of ways to get sum t using i dice. The base case would be dp[0][0] = 1, since zero dice can make sum 0 in one way. Then for each die, we can look at all possible previous sums and add the possibilities from adding a new die.

Wait, for each die, we can iterate from 1 to k. So for the i-th die, each possible face value adds to the previous sum. For example, to compute dp[i][target], we look at dp[i-1][target - face_value] for each face_value in 1..k, as long as target - face_value is non-negative.

So the recurrence would be something like:
dp[i][t] = sum of dp[i-1][t - face] for face from 1 to k, if t - face >=0.

class Solution {
public:
    int numRollsToTarget(int n, int k, int target) {
        const int MOD = 1e9 +7;
        vector<int> prev(target + 1, 0);
        prev[0] = 1;
        for (int die = 1; die <= n; ++die) {
            vector<int> curr(target + 1, 0);
            for (int t = 1; t <= target; ++t) {
                for (int face = 1; face <= k; ++face) {
                    if (t - face >= 0) {
                        curr[t] = (curr[t] + prev[t - face]) % MOD;
                    }
                }
            }
            prev = move(curr);
        }
        return prev[target];
    }
};

public:

};

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int numRollsToTarget(int n, int k, int target) {
        const int MOD = 1000000007;
        vector<int> prev(target + 1, 0);
        prev[0] = 1;
        for (int die = 1; die <= n; ++die) {
            vector<int> curr(target + 1, 0);
            for (int t = 1; t <= target; ++t) {
                for (int face = 1; face <= k; ++face) {
                    if (t - face >= 0) {
                        curr[t] = (curr[t] + prev[t - face]) % MOD;
                    }
                }
            }
            prev = move(curr);
        }
        return prev[target];
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n * k * target)
space complexity: O(target)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses dynamic programming with two 1D arrays to track possible sums. For each die, iterates through all possible target sums and face values, accumulating valid combinations. Efficient for given constraints (n, k ≤ 30, target ≤ 1000). Time complexity is cubic in worst case but manageable due to small input limits. Space optimized to linear in target.
